explanation: The requirement states that the system must not allow (i.e., must prevent) a healthcare professional operator from erasing the alarm system log. The atomic propositions are 'user_equalTo_operator' (a healthcare professional is operating the system) and 'eraseLog' (erasure of the contents of the alarm system log). The correct formalization is: at all times, if the user is an operator, then eraseLog must not happen. This is captured by 'G (user_equalTo_operator -> (! eraseLog))', which prohibits the occurrence of eraseLog whenever the healthcare professional operator is present.

The natural language requirement specifies that the healthcare professional operator (user) must not be permitted to erase the contents of the alarm system log. This means that whenever a healthcare professional is operating the system (user_equalTo_operator is true), 'eraseLog' (erasure of the alarm log contents) must never happen. This is a safety (forbidden action) property. The best way to capture this is to say that it is always the case that if the user is an operator, then eraseLog is never true. This is expressed by the LTL formula 'G(user_equalTo_operator -> (!eraseLog))'. This uses template 38 (G (bool_exp1 -> (G (! bool_exp3)))), where bool_exp1 is 'user_equalTo_operator' and bool_exp3 is 'eraseLog'. N_DURATION is not needed for this template.

The requirement is that the healthcare professional operator ('user_equalTo_operator') shall NOT be permitted to erase the contents of the alarm system log ('eraseLog'). This means that at all times, if the user is an operator, 'eraseLog' should never be true. To express this restriction in LTL, we use the template for 'Globally, if user_equalTo_operator, then eraseLog is never true.' This matches template 38, 'G (bool_exp1 -> (G (! bool_exp3)))', where 'bool_exp1' is 'user_equalTo_operator' and 'bool_exp3' is 'eraseLog'. This ensures that as long as a healthcare professional is the operator, the erase action on the alarm system log cannot occur at any point in the future.

The requirement specifies that the healthcare professional operator (user == operator) must never be able to erase the contents of the alarm system log (eraseLog). To capture this, we ensure that in all states where the user is a healthcare professional operator, erasing the log is forbidden. Thus, globally, whenever the user_equalTo_operator holds, eraseLog must not occur. The LTL formula expresses this as: 'Globally, if user_equalTo_operator then not eraseLog.'

The requirement is that the healthcare professional operator must not be able to erase the alarm system log. The atomic propositions available include 'eraseLog' (indicates erasure of log) and 'user_equalTo_operator' (a healthcare professional is operating the system). We want to express that whenever a healthcare professional is operating the system, the erase action does NOT happen at any time. This is best captured by: G((user_equalTo_operator) -> (G (!eraseLog))) -- or in the template, using: (G (bool_exp1 -> (G (! bool_exp3)))), where bool_exp1 is user_equalTo_operator and bool_exp3 is eraseLog. However, that precise pattern is not in the available templates, but we can use a slightly less strict but still correct one: G((user_equalTo_operator) -> (!eraseLog)), which ensures that at any time a healthcare professional operator is present, erasure does not occur in that state. This directly matches template 98: G (bool_exp1 -> (! bool_exp3)), with bool_exp1=user_equalTo_operator and bool_exp3=eraseLog. This template ensures that whenever a healthcare professional operates the system, it is not possible to erase the log.

The natural language requirement states that the system shall prohibit (not permit) the erasure of the alarm system log by a healthcare professional operator. Given the atomic propositions, 'eraseLog' indicates erasure of the alarm system log, and 'user_equalTo_operator' indicates a healthcare professional is operating the system. Thus, whenever 'user_equalTo_operator' is true, 'eraseLog' must never occur (be true). The appropriate template is the 'globally if P then always not Q': G (bool_exp1 -> G (!bool_exp3)). However, since the template library only provides variants with G (bool_exp1 -> (! bool_exp3)), the closest is template 98 (G (bool_exp1 -> (! bool_exp3))), ensuring that whenever the user is an operator, eraseLog is never allowed at any point.

The requirement states that a healthcare professional operator should never be able to erase the contents of the alarm system log. Here, the atomic propositions are 'eraseLog' (indicates erasure of the contents of the alarm system log) and 'user_equalTo_operator' (indicates a healthcare professional is operating the system). So, we must ensure that whenever the operator is using the system, 'eraseLog' does not occur at any time. This is best captured using the template 'Globally, whenever bool_exp1 holds, not bool_exp3 holds'. That is, globally (at all times), if a healthcare professional is the operator, then erasure of the log should not happen. This expresses a strict safety property excluding all such erasure events for these users.

The requirement states that a healthcare professional operator (indicated by 'user_equalTo_operator') must not be allowed to erase the contents of the alarm system log (indicated by 'eraseLog'). In LTL, this can be represented as: 'It is always the case that if a healthcare professional is operating the system, then eraseLog is never true.' Thus, the globally operator 'G' is used for always, combined with an implication (if operator, then not action). Here, the correct template is: G (bool_exp1 -> (! bool_exp3)), with bool_exp1 = user_equalTo_operator, and bool_exp3 = eraseLog. The other placeholders are unnecessary for this property. The N_DURATION is not required for this template.

The natural language requirement states that when a healthcare professional is operating the system (indicated by 'user_equalTo_operator'), the system must not permit erasure of the alarm system log ('eraseLog'). This describes a state safety invariant: whenever a healthcare professional is operating, the erase log action cannot occur. In LTL, this is best captured as a global property: globally, it is never true that both 'user_equalTo_operator' and 'eraseLog' hold at the same time. Or equivalently, globally, 'user_equalTo_operator' implies NOT 'eraseLog'. Thus, we use template 38: G (bool_exp1 -> (G (! bool_exp3))). Here, bool_exp1 = user_equalTo_operator, and bool_exp3 = eraseLog.

The input requirement states that the system must not allow the healthcare professional operator to erase the alarm system log. The atomic propositions available are 'eraseLog' (indicates erasure of the contents of the alarm system log) and 'user_equalTo_operator' (indicates a healthcare professional is operating the system). To formalize this, we want to ensure that whenever the operator is the user, it is always the case that 'eraseLog' is false (i.e., it never occurs). This is best captured by the template 'G (bool_exp1 -> (! bool_exp3))', where bool_exp1 is 'user_equalTo_operator' and bool_exp3 is 'eraseLog'. This expresses that globally, if the user is an operator, 'eraseLog' must never be true, directly matching the requirement.
